---
title: "Why Autograd Needs Floats, Not Ints"
subtitle: "Calculus is continuous — and integer tensors quietly kill the gradient signal"
date: "2026-04-21"
image: "images/autograd-floats-img.png"
title-block-banner: "images/autograd-floats-img.png"
categories: [Deep Learning]
draft: true
status: evergreen
---

## Introduction

Set `requires_grad=True` on an integer tensor in PyTorch and you get a runtime error: only floating point and complex tensors can require gradients. The fix is one keyword argument. The reason behind the rule is more interesting than the fix — it reflects a structural fact about gradient descent rather than an arbitrary framework choice, and it explains a few related failures that aren't loud enough to throw an error of their own.

Two things have to hold for gradient descent to work: the loss has to change continuously with the parameter, and the resulting change has to have a usable sign and magnitude. Integer parameters fail both, in two independent ways. The chain-rule mechanics this post relies on are covered in [Automatic Differentiation Demystified](../mlsys/automatic-differentiation.qmd).

## What the Derivative Sees on Floats and Integers

Plotting the same function over floats and over integers makes the contrast immediate. Below is $f(x) = x^2/10$ as a continuous curve, and the same function under integer rounding, $\lfloor x^2/10 \rfloor$. The slider sets $x$; the toggle switches between the two regimes.

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| label: fig-autograd-widget
#| fig-cap: "Drag the slider to move **x**. Flip **Continuous Mode** off to see the same function snapped to integer outputs — the tangent flattens, the gradient reads 0.00, and STATUS changes from OPTIMIZING to STUCK. That flat gradient is exactly what autograd would hand back to your optimizer."

import numpy as np
from bokeh.plotting import figure
from bokeh.models import (
    ColumnDataSource, Slider, Switch, NumericInput,
    Div, Label, CustomJS,
)
from bokeh.layouts import column, row
from bokeh.embed import components
from bokeh.resources import CDN
from IPython.display import HTML

x0 = 5.0
f = lambda x: x**2 / 10
fp = lambda x: x / 5

xs = np.linspace(-5, 5, 500)
curve_src = ColumnDataSource(dict(x=xs, y=f(xs)))
point_src = ColumnDataSource(dict(x=[x0], y=[f(x0)]))

L = 1.3
tangent_src = ColumnDataSource(dict(
    x=[x0 - L, x0 + L],
    y=[f(x0) - fp(x0) * L, f(x0) + fp(x0) * L],
))

p = figure(
    height=320, width=680,
    x_range=(-5.5, 5.5), y_range=(-0.4, 3.0),
    toolbar_location=None,
    outline_line_color="#e5e7eb",
    background_fill_color="#fafafa",
)
p.grid.visible = False
p.axis.visible = False

p.line("x", "y", source=curve_src, line_width=2.5, color="#1f6feb")
p.line("x", "y", source=tangent_src, line_dash="dashed", line_width=2.5, color="#1a7f37")
p.scatter("x", "y", source=point_src, size=8,
          fill_color="#1a7f37", line_color="#1a7f37")

point_label = Label(
    x=x0, y=f(x0) - 0.5, text=f"POINT (X={x0:.1f})",
    text_font_size="10pt", text_font_style="bold", text_align="center",
    border_line_color="#d0d7de", border_line_width=1,
    background_fill_color="white", background_fill_alpha=0.95,
    padding=6,
)
p.add_layout(point_label)

chart_label = Div(text="<span style='color:#57606a; font-size:12px;'>↗ Continuous Flow</span>")
caption = Div(text=(
    "<div style='font-size:13px; color:#57606a; padding:4px 0;'>"
    "Continuous Gradient: the slope changes smoothly. Gradient descent "
    "'feels' the curve and knows which direction to move."
    "</div>"
))

def stat(label, value, color="#24292f"):
    return (
        f"<div style='display:inline-block; margin-right:28px; font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;'>"
        f"<div style='font-size:10px; color:#57606a; letter-spacing:0.7px;'>{label}</div>"
        f"<div style='font-size:15px; font-weight:700; color:{color};'>{value}</div>"
        f"</div>"
    )

stats_x = Div(text=stat("INPUT X", f"{x0:.2f}"))
stats_y = Div(text=stat("OUTPUT Y", f"{f(x0):.2f}"))
stats_g = Div(text=stat("GRADIENT dy/dx", f"{fp(x0):.2f}"))
stats_s = Div(text=stat("STATUS", "OPTIMIZING", color="#1a7f37"))

x_slider = Slider(start=-5, end=5, step=0.01, value=x0, title="Input (x)", width=280)
x_input = NumericInput(value=x0, low=-5, high=5, mode="float", width=70)
mode = Switch(active=True)
mode_label = Div(text="<span style='font-size:13px; margin-right:8px;'>Continuous Mode</span>")

cb = CustomJS(
    args=dict(
        curve=curve_src, point=point_src, tangent=tangent_src,
        slider=x_slider, mode=mode,
        stats_x=stats_x, stats_y=stats_y, stats_g=stats_g, stats_s=stats_s,
        caption=caption, chart_label=chart_label, point_label=point_label,
        x_input=x_input,
    ),
    code="""
    const x = slider.value;
    const cont = mode.active;
    const N = 500;
    const xs = new Array(N);
    const ys = new Array(N);
    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {
        const v = -5 + (10 * i) / (N - 1);
        xs[i] = v;
        ys[i] = cont ? (v*v/10) : Math.floor(v*v/10);
    }
    curve.data = { x: xs, y: ys };

    const y_at_x = cont ? (x*x/10) : Math.floor(x*x/10);
    point.data = { x: [x], y: [y_at_x] };

    const slope = cont ? (x/5) : 0;
    const L = 1.3;
    tangent.data = {
        x: [x - L, x + L],
        y: [y_at_x - slope*L, y_at_x + slope*L],
    };

    point_label.x = x;
    point_label.y = y_at_x - 0.5;
    point_label.text = `POINT (X=${x.toFixed(1)})`;

    const stat = (lbl, val, color) =>
        `<div style='display:inline-block; margin-right:28px; font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;'>
           <div style='font-size:10px; color:#57606a; letter-spacing:0.7px;'>${lbl}</div>
           <div style='font-size:15px; font-weight:700; color:${color || "#24292f"};'>${val}</div>
         </div>`;
    stats_x.text = stat("INPUT X", x.toFixed(2));
    stats_y.text = stat("OUTPUT Y", y_at_x.toFixed(2));
    stats_g.text = stat("GRADIENT dy/dx", cont ? slope.toFixed(2) : "0.00",
                        cont ? "#24292f" : "#cf222e");
    stats_s.text = stat("STATUS", cont ? "OPTIMIZING" : "STUCK",
                        cont ? "#1a7f37" : "#cf222e");

    caption.text = cont
        ? "<div style='font-size:13px; color:#57606a; padding:4px 0;'>Continuous Gradient: the slope changes smoothly. Gradient descent 'feels' the curve and knows which direction to move.</div>"
        : "<div style='font-size:13px; color:#57606a; padding:4px 0;'>Discrete Gradient: the slope is zero on every tread. Gradient descent has no signal to follow — learning stalls.</div>";

    chart_label.text = cont
        ? "<span style='color:#57606a; font-size:12px;'>↗ Continuous Flow</span>"
        : "<span style='color:#57606a; font-size:12px;'>▣ Discrete Steps</span>";

    if (Math.abs(x_input.value - x) > 1e-6) x_input.value = x;
    """,
)

x_slider.js_on_change("value", cb)
mode.js_on_change("active", cb)
x_input.js_on_change("value", CustomJS(
    args=dict(slider=x_slider),
    code="if (Math.abs(slider.value - cb_obj.value) > 1e-6) slider.value = cb_obj.value;",
))

layout = column(
    row(stats_x, stats_y, stats_g, stats_s),
    caption,
    chart_label,
    p,
    row(x_slider, x_input, mode_label, mode),
)

script, div = components(layout)
HTML(f"{CDN.render()}{div}{script}")
```

On the float curve the tangent tilts smoothly with slope $x/5$. On the staircase, the slope is zero on every tread and undefined at every riser; those are the only two values the derivative can take. This is a property of the function itself, not the visualization. The derivative at a point is the limit of the slope as the neighborhood around it shrinks, and on an integer-valued function every neighborhood resolves to either a flat tread or a vertical jump. There is no third case to converge to, and a gradient computed at either of these points carries no information about which direction lowers the loss.

## Integer Casts and the Chain Rule

A network is a long chain of operations. Reverse-mode autograd computes the gradient of the loss with respect to any parameter by multiplying local gradients along that chain (covered in [Automatic Differentiation Demystified](../mlsys/automatic-differentiation.qmd)), so one zero anywhere in the product zeros the entire product. A single integer-rounding step in the forward pass — explicit (`.to(torch.int)`, `torch.round`) or implicit (`argmax`, a boolean mask from `>` or `<`) — has local gradient zero almost everywhere, and zeros the gradient for every parameter upstream of it.

```python
import torch
x = torch.tensor([1.5], requires_grad=True)
y = (x * 2).to(torch.int64).to(torch.float32)   # round-trip through int
loss = (y - 5.0) ** 2
loss.backward()
print(x.grad)   # tensor([0.])
```

The loss computes, the backward pass runs without error, and `x.grad` is zero. Training will proceed; the upstream parameters won't move. The structure is the same as a dead ReLU, except a dead ReLU only kills the gradient for negative pre-activations — an integer cast represents a function with no nonzero gradient anywhere it's defined.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Integer Operations in the Forward Pass Are Gradient Walls
`.to(torch.int)`, `torch.round`, `torch.floor`, `torch.ceil`, `argmax`, and boolean masks from `>` / `<` all have zero or undefined local gradient. Each one zeros the gradient for every parameter upstream of it, and the loss continues to compute as if nothing were wrong.
:::

## The Update-Granularity Problem

The second reason is independent of calculus. The SGD update is $w \leftarrow w - \eta \cdot g$, and $\eta \cdot g$ is typically on the order of $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ per step. On a float weight that nudges it; on an integer weight `5 - 0.0015` rounds straight back to `5`. The weight doesn't move until a gradient arrives that's large enough to round to at least 1, which is orders of magnitude above any stable learning rate.

This is why low-precision training (bfloat16, fp8) still uses *floating* point. The issue isn't the bit count, it's continuity: a float grid is dense enough that the rounding error stays below the update magnitude. An integer grid isn't.

## When Integer Tensors Are Safe

The rule is about the gradient path, not about integer tensors as such. Integer tensors appear in every real model and they're fine, as long as nothing on the gradient path passes through them:

- **Embedding indices** (`nn.Embedding`): the integer selects a row; the gradient flows back to the row's float weights, not the index.
- **Class labels** (`nn.CrossEntropyLoss` targets): a lookup into the logits, not a parameter.
- **Masks, `gather` indices, segment IDs**: data, not values on the gradient path.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Integer Tensors Are Fine as Addresses, Never as Values
When a tensor's dtype is integer, autograd treats it as an index and drops it from the graph. That is correct for lookups and labels, and silently wrong if it happens to a weight or an activation.
:::

## Quantization and the Straight-Through Estimator

The obvious counterexample is quantized networks, which use integer weights at inference. They train as floats. In quantization-aware training the weights are kept in `float32`; the forward pass simulates the quantization by rounding, but the backward pass replaces the round's true local gradient (zero almost everywhere) with 1 — the **straight-through estimator** (Bengio et al., 2013). Gradients flow through the rounding as if it weren't there.

The STE is not calculus. It is a known approximation, used because the true derivative is what the rest of this post has been about: useless. It is the workaround for the rule, not an exception to it.

## Key Takeaways

1. **Derivatives are defined on continuous spaces.** On an integer-valued function, the derivative is zero on every flat segment and undefined at every jump, so autograd has nothing useful to compute and pass to the optimizer.
2. **One integer cast zeros the gradient for everything upstream of it.** The chain rule multiplies local gradients along the network; a single zero in the product is enough to kill the whole thing. The loss continues to compute, so the failure is silent.
3. **Even setting calculus aside, the SGD update fails on integers.** Step sizes of $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$ round straight back to the previous integer; the weight never moves.
4. **Integer tensors are fine off the gradient path.** Embedding indices, class labels, and masks are all valid uses. Autograd refuses `requires_grad=True` on integer dtypes precisely to keep them off the path.
5. **Quantization works by training in float and quantizing afterwards.** The straight-through estimator substitutes a usable gradient for the round's true (useless) one — a known approximation, not a counterexample to the rule.

## Resources

1. [**Automatic Differentiation Demystified**](../mlsys/automatic-differentiation.qmd) — Companion post on how autograd actually builds and traverses the graph; covers chain rule mechanics, activation memory, and gradient checkpointing.
2. [**PyTorch Autograd Mechanics**](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/autograd.html) — How `requires_grad` propagates and how non-differentiable ops are handled.
3. [**Bengio, Léonard, Courville (2013) — Estimating or Propagating Gradients Through Stochastic Neurons**](https://arxiv.org/abs/1308.3432) — The straight-through estimator.
